planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plantain-leaf sedge grows naturally in moist, rocky, north-facing woodland slopes in rich, deep soil and requires similar conditions in the garden.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plantain-leaf sedge is the most architecturally distinctive of all the native shade sedges, immediately recognizable by its unusually broad, pleated, dark green leaves (1–2 inches wide) with prominent parallel veins resembling plantain (Plantago) leaves — a completely different aesthetic from typical narrow-leaved sedges. The leaves remain evergreen or semi-evergreen through winter, providing year-round ground-level interest in the woodland garden. Burgundy-red stems bear small, elegant fruiting spikes in early spring before most other plants emerge. It grows naturally on moist, north-facing, rocky woodland slopes in the Appalachians and eastern US. Slower to spread than some native sedges but forms a beautiful, refined groundcover in appropriate conditions. An outstanding accent plant for the front of a shaded border or as a specimen among ferns and hostas.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; fringetrees are dioecious — female plants produce ornamental black berries if a male is nearby, though both sexes bloom spectacularly.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; white fringetree is one of the most spectacular spring-flowering native plants in North America, producing masses of drooping, fringe-like, pure white flower panicles (each petal strap-shaped and narrow, like white fringe) that completely cover the plant in late spring, appearing briefly after the leaves emerge. The effect of a fully bloomed fringetree — a large, billowing cloud of pure white flowers — is extraordinary. Female plants produce dark blue-purple olive-like berries in fall that are important food for birds; the leaves turn clear yellow in fall. Remarkably adaptable — tolerating partial shade, moderate drought, clay soils, and a very wide hardiness range (zone 3–9). One of the most valuable and versatile native large shrubs or small trees for the American garden, yet widely underplanted. Slow to establish but very long-lived.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; small-flower pawpaw tolerates significantly drier conditions and more shade than the common pawpaw (A. triloba) and naturally grows in the understory of longleaf pine flatwoods and dry woodlands.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade — small-flower pawpaw is the native pawpaw of the southeastern coastal plain, growing in the dry understory of longleaf pine flatwoods and open, sandy woodlands from Virginia to Florida and west to Louisiana. It is more shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant than the common pawpaw (A. triloba) and produces smaller but similarly flavored fruits — small oblong yellow-green pawpaws 1–2 inches long with the characteristic sweet, custard-like tropical flavor of the familiar large pawpaw. Like all Asimina species, it is the exclusive larval host plant for the zebra swallowtail butterfly, which depends entirely on Asimina for its caterpillars. The small maroon flowers in early spring resemble the larger common pawpaw flowers. A valuable native plant for the shaded, dry woodland garden in the Southeast, supporting both edible fruit production and butterfly habitat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; vernal witch hazel tolerates moist soil and periodic flooding along streams better than most other witch hazels.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade — vernal witch hazel is the earliest-blooming native Hamamelis species, opening its strap-petaled flowers in January through March while the plant is still dormant, even in the coldest conditions of zone 4–5. The flowers come in shades from pale yellow to orange to deep brick-red (dark orange-red in the commonly grown form) and have a sweet, spicy fragrance. Unlike the common witch hazel (H. virginiana), which blooms in fall, H. vernalis blooms in late winter and is the best native species for early-season pollinator value — providing nectar for early queen bumblebees and native mining bees on warm winter days. It is native to the gravel bars and rocky streambanks of the Ozarks and tolerates moist to wet conditions. The fall foliage is brilliant orange-yellow.

planting.note: Plant fresh nuts or container-grown seedlings in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun; American chestnut requires two or more trees for cross-pollination to set nuts. Consider blight-resistant hybrid trees from the American Chestnut Foundation for greater long-term survival.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, deep soil in full sun — the American chestnut was once the dominant tree of the eastern forest, comprising 25% of all trees from New England to the Deep South and providing the primary mast crop sustaining deer, turkeys, bears, and passenger pigeons across millions of acres. The introduction of chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica) from Asia in the early 1900s killed an estimated 4 billion trees within 50 years in the most catastrophic ecological disaster in American history. The root system of most trees survives and sends up shoots that grow to 10–20 feet before dying of blight — these sprouts can produce a small crop of nuts. The American Chestnut Foundation has developed blight-tolerant hybrid trees (about 94% American chestnut genetics) and restoration planting is actively underway. Planting American chestnut seedlings or blight-resistant hybrids is a meaningful conservation act; the trees grow fast, produce sweet nuts, and support 125 species of caterpillars.
